## Working with `ail` in this project

This project uses **ail** — a YAML-orchestrated pipeline runtime that
fires a declared sequence of steps after every human prompt, before
control returns to the user. Pipelines live in `.ail.yaml` at the repo
root or under `.ail/`.

If you see a `.ail.yaml` here, ail is the harness for this project:
the human's prompt becomes the implicit `invocation` step zero, and
every authored step in the pipeline runs after it. You are likely
**inside** a pipeline run right now.

### When the user asks you to write or change a pipeline

The AIL language is not in your training data. **Read the spec first**
— a stale guess will produce syntactically plausible but invalid YAML.

```bash
ail spec --format compact         # authoring reference (~40-50k tokens),
                                  # excludes ail's internals + roadmap
ail spec --list                   # one-line table of contents
ail spec --section s05            # deep-dive a specific section
ail spec --section s11,s27,s29    # multiple sections at once
```

`--format compact` is the canonical reference for *writing* pipelines:
it's generated at build time from the section files and contains every
rule and example you need, with implementation internals stripped out.
Pull it before authoring; quote section IDs (`s11`, `s27`, etc.) when
explaining your work.

### The validation loop

Treat these two commands as the build/test cycle for any `.ail.yaml`
change:

```bash
ail validate --pipeline .ail.yaml          # parse + validate, no LLM calls
ail materialize --pipeline .ail.yaml       # print fully-resolved YAML
                                           # (FROM-inheritance flattened,
                                           # origin comments)
```

A pipeline that doesn't `validate` is broken. Always run both before
claiming a change works. Add `--dry-run` to a normal `ail` invocation
to walk the resolved pipeline end-to-end without making LLM calls.

### Running pipelines

```bash
ail "<prompt>"                              # run the discovered pipeline
ail "<prompt>" --pipeline path/to.ail.yaml  # explicit pipeline
ail "<prompt>" --dry-run                    # full resolution, no LLM calls
ail "<prompt>" --output-format json         # NDJSON event stream for tools
```

If no pipeline is found, ail runs in **passthrough mode** — your prompt
is forwarded to the runner as a single step with no orchestration. This
is the safe zero-config default.

### When NOT to bypass ail

If the project has a pipeline, prefer changes that go *through* it —
add or modify steps, write a skill, adjust an `on_result` branch — over
one-shot direct edits that the pipeline would have caught (test gates,
security audits, format checks). The pipeline is the project's
contract for what "done" means after a prompt.

### Quick reference

| Need | Command |
|---|---|
| Learn the language | `ail spec --format compact` |
| Browse sections | `ail spec --list` |
| Deep-dive a topic | `ail spec --section sNN` |
| Validate a YAML edit | `ail validate --pipeline <path>` |
| Inspect resolved YAML | `ail materialize --pipeline <path>` |
| Dry-run end to end | `ail "<prompt>" --dry-run` |
| Scaffold a workspace | `ail init` |
